# Workshop Approach

<!-- 
Based on: nls-workshop-focused.qmd presentation
Purpose: Document how workshops surface AI opportunities
Key insight: Interactive sessions beat surveys for finding real needs
-->

## Why Workshops Work

- Face-to-face discussion surfaces hidden pain points
- Group dynamics reveal shared challenges
- Real-time clarification of technical feasibility
- Builds buy-in through participation

<!-- Compare to other methods: -->
<!-- - Surveys: low response, abstract answers -->
<!-- - Top-down: misses ground-level reality -->
<!-- - Tech-first: solutions looking for problems -->

## Workshop Structure

### Timing (90-120 minutes typical)
- 30 min: Presentation/context setting
- 15 min: Individual reflection
- 30 min: Small group discussion
- 30 min: Full group sharing
- 15 min: Next steps

<!-- Variations: -->
<!-- - Half-day deep dives -->
<!-- - Series of shorter sessions -->
<!-- - Department-specific vs cross-functional -->

## Key Concepts to Present

### The Ferrari vs Honda Civic Analogy
<!-- From NLS workshop slides -->
- Frontier models = Ferrari (impressive but high-maintenance)
- Specialized models = Honda Civic (practical and reliable)
- Libraries need Civics, not Ferraris

<!-- Visual: Include slide_7_0.png or similar -->

### Four Ways Framework
<!-- Present with concrete examples -->
1. Transform (OCR, transcription)
2. Extract & Describe (entities, summaries)
3. Organize (classify, categorize)
4. Find (search, discover)

<!-- Use actual library examples for each -->

### The "Boring but Valuable" Principle
- Most impactful AI = least glamorous tasks
- Repetitive decisions that consume time
- Clear categories and outcomes
- 80% accuracy threshold

<!-- Examples that resonate: -->
<!-- - "Is this a letter or report?" -->
<!-- - "What language is this?" -->
<!-- - "Is this suitable for public access?" -->

## Workshop Activities

### Activity 1: Task Inventory (15 min)
<!-- Individual reflection -->

Questions to prompt:
- What repetitive decision do you make most often?
- What task has the biggest backlog?
- What would you delegate if you had an assistant?

<!-- Provide worksheet with: -->
- Task description
- Frequency (daily/weekly/monthly)
- Time per item
- Number of people doing similar work

### Activity 2: Reality Check (10 min)
<!-- Small groups -->

Assess each task:
- Clear categories? (2-5, 6-10, subjective)
- Existing examples? (thousands, hundreds, few)
- 80% accuracy useful? (definitely, probably, no)

<!-- Red/yellow/green scoring -->

### Activity 3: Impact Assessment (15 min)
<!-- Small groups continue -->

For promising tasks:
- Hours saved per week?
- What would you do with that time?
- Concerns about AI doing this?
- Who else would benefit?

<!-- Capture on shared boards/slides -->

### Activity 4: Sharing and Patterns (30 min)
<!-- Full group -->

- Each group shares top opportunity
- Identify common themes
- Map to four-way framework
- Discuss feasibility

<!-- Facilitator captures on whiteboard -->
<!-- Look for cross-department applications -->

## Facilitation Techniques

### Setting the Right Tone
- "No idea too boring"
- "Think practical not fancy"
- "What annoys you daily?"
- Emphasize time savings not job replacement

### Managing Dynamics
- Mix departments for cross-pollination
- Pair technical and non-technical staff
- Encourage specific examples
- Redirect from "wouldn't it be cool if..."

### Common Challenges
- **"Everything needs 100% accuracy"**
  - Response: Discuss review workflows
  - Show 80/20 impact examples
  
- **"We don't have the data"**
  - Response: Often you do (past decisions = data)
  - Start with what exists
  
- **"This will replace jobs"**
  - Response: Focus on augmentation
  - Highlight higher-value work enabled

## Outcomes to Expect

### Typical Discoveries
- Same pain points across departments
- Hidden data resources
- Quick wins vs long-term projects
- Champions emerge naturally

### Common Task Categories
<!-- From NLS experience -->
- Document type classification
- Language identification  
- Quality assessment
- Sensitivity screening
- Subject categorization
- Duplicate detection

### Prioritization Criteria
1. Impact (hours saved × people affected)
2. Feasibility (data exists, clear outcomes)
3. Reusability (multiple collections/departments)
4. Risk (low consequence of errors)

## Post-Workshop Actions

### Immediate (same day)
- Thank participants
- Share summary of ideas
- Identify quick wins
- Schedule follow-ups

### Short-term (1 week)
- Detailed assessment forms for top candidates
- Data availability audit
- Stakeholder mapping
- Pilot project proposals

### Long-term (1 month)
- Select 2-3 pilots
- Form working groups
- Develop success metrics
- Communication plan

## Materials and Resources

### Presentation Materials
<!-- Link to or embed from workshops folder -->
- Slide deck template
- Ferrari vs Honda Civic slides
- Four ways framework visuals
- Case study examples

### Worksheets
- Task inventory form
- Reality check checklist
- Impact assessment grid
- Prioritization matrix

### Facilitator Resources
- Question prompts
- Time management guide
- Common objections and responses
- Success stories from other libraries

## Adapting for Your Context

### Institutional Variations
- Academic libraries: Research support focus
- Public libraries: Community service angle
- Archives: Preservation and access
- Museums: Object description and interpretation

### Scale Considerations
- Small institutions: Focus on consortium opportunities
- Large institutions: Department-specific sessions
- Consortia: Shared challenges and solutions

### Cultural Factors
- Risk tolerance levels
- Innovation readiness
- Change management needs
- Union considerations

## Measuring Workshop Success

### Immediate Indicators
- Engagement level
- Quality of examples shared
- Number of viable opportunities identified
- Participant enthusiasm

### Long-term Metrics
- Pilots launched from workshop ideas
- Time savings achieved
- Cross-department collaboration
- Continued engagement

## Next Steps

<!-- Link to assessment tool chapter -->
<!-- How to gather detailed requirements -->
<!-- Moving from workshop to implementation -->

---

<!-- 
Additional resources to develop:
- Video examples of workshop segments
- Downloadable slide templates
- Facilitation training materials
- Workshop evaluation forms
- Case studies from different institution types
-->

<!-- 
Quotes to add from participants:
- "I never thought of that as AI"
- "We've been doing this manually for years"
- "80% would be life-changing"
-->
